body_txt: Hyenas are frequently portrayed as evil in fiction. They are often depicted as either insane Ax-Crazy sadists , as stupid Dirty Cowards , as a horrific Monster Clown , as a Super-Persistent Predator , or as the occasional Terrible Trio . The majority of the time, however, they're depicted as dirty, smelly Villainous Gluttons . These hyenas also have a fondness for laughing and a fear (and hatred) of lions . Note that this is a relatively recent phenomenon, effectively jumpstarted by The Lion King (1994) , as prior to that, hyenas were infrequently used in western media, and when they did pop up, they were never given the spotlight and did little more than laugh a lot . Due to the omnipresence of this trope in the last few decades, good hyenas in fiction pretty much only exist as a deliberate subversion. Thankfully, these have become slightly more common in recent years, even though it is still somewhat rare. This trope does not only apply to actual hyenas, but humans or other creatures that use hyena symbolism; comparing a character with an unpleasant laugh to a hyena is particularly common in fiction, to the point that there's a trope named after it . Fictional hyenas are mostly spotted hyenas , or at least draw primarily on them — spots, social behavior, competition with lions, and the famous laughing noise are all unique to them; striped hyenas might show up if the story is set in Arabia or India, while brown hyenas and aardwolves (the latter being a purely insect-eating species) are still practically unheard of. Although when these other species do appear, they aren't always portrayed as negatively as the spotted hyena. Ironically, the spotted hyena is the largest, strongest, and smartest hyena, very rarely scavenging (in contrast to their solitary striped and brown cousins), and living in large clans with a clear hierarchy, being the only extant species to be a true apex predator; lions are more likely to take kills from them than the other way around, in most cases. Despite this, they are frequently portrayed as scavengers, and often contemptible or evil ones . Their bad reputation may in part be because the hyena's signature trait — that laughing sound — is actually only made by the spotted ones. This doesn't exactly help their case, either; as hyenas tend to laugh in stressful or exciting situations, with hunting being one for obvious reasons, the imagery of a pack laughing as they brutally tear their prey apart only furthered humanity's negative opinions of them. Their strange, seemingly chimeric appearance, especially from the perspective of Westerners more familiar with canids and felids, also doesn't help. Although hyenas are closest to mongooses and civets, and by extension cats , they look and partially behave very much like dogs (a case of convergent evolution), yet they still sport stripes or spots like their feline and viverrid relatives, and the other three (non-spotted) species also have large, bat-like ears (which can sport a lot of ear notches from various battles) and frizzy manes running down their backs (or in the case of the brown hyena, covering their entire body), bringing to mind fright wigs or Delinquent Hair , thus giving them a very gritty but also garish, street punk-like appearance. Animators will often not do their research and/or deliberately combine elements from different hyena species (usually the spotted and striped variety), giving them an even freakier appearance. If we get a positive potrayal of a hyena, they might be shown as Ugly Cute for much the same reasons. Compare Our Gnolls Are Different , as Dungeons & Dragons -inspired gnolls are very often depicted as bloodthirsty hyena-men. Contrast King of Beasts , Panthera Awesome , Noble Wolf , and Heroic Dog since hyenas are often made out as the Unpleasant Animal Counterpart to big cats and wild dogs. See also Humorous Hyena , which may overlap in the case of Laughably Evil hyenas.
